preFix.title: Fitting a pelmet board above a window
preFix.subtitle: Fix a timber pelmet board to the wall above a window to conceal a curtain track
preFix.excerpt: How to measure, cut, and fix a timber pelmet board above a window using a pair of right-angle metal brackets, set the board at the correct projection from the wall to clear the curtain heading, and finish the edges and face ready for painting or fabric wrapping.
preFix.sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. British Interior Design Association technical notes. Designers' Guild installation guidance.

content.text: needs to project far enough from the wall to clear the stack of the curtain when it is pulled back: typically 120 to 150 mm. It must also sit high enough above the window opening for the curtain heading to clear the underside of the board (allow 25 mm minimum clearance). Both dimensions should be confirmed before cutting any timber.

Measure the length of the curtain track plus 50 mm overhang each side (to cover the track end-stops). This gives the board length. For the projection, measure from the wall face to the outermost ring or glider on the track and add 25 mm clearance. Cut the main board to length and width from 18 mm MDF or planed softwood. Cut two
to the board depth and 18 mm shorter than the projection (to account for the board thickness).
Step 2, fix the brackets to the wall
Mark the position of the two

on the wall: 150 mm from each end of the board position, at the correct height above the track. Check both bracket marks are at the same height using the spirit level. Drill masonry fixing holes and insert wall plugs, or drive screws into the stud on a timber-frame wall. Fix the brackets with No. 10 × 50 mm screws. Check both bracket horizontal arms are at the same level.
Step 3, assemble and fit the board

Glue and pin the return pieces to each end of the main board using wood glue and 40 mm oval nails or pin nails driven through the main board face into the return end grain. Set any nail heads below the surface with a nail punch. Sand the face and edges smooth. Lift the assembled board onto the angle brackets and fix from below with No. 8 × 25 mm screws driven up through the bracket arm into the underside of the board.

Fill any nail holes, gaps at the wall, and bracket reveal with a flexible filler. Sand flush when dry. Prime all surfaces including the back face: MDF and bare timber both must be primed before painting or the topcoat will sink unevenly into the surface. If the pelmet is to be fabric-wrapped rather than painted, apply PVA glue to all face surfaces and wrap the fabric over the board, pulling it taut and stapling to the back face.

A shaped pelmet: curved at the lower face: is cut on a jigsaw and the curved face is sanded smooth before priming. The return pieces remain rectangular and are attached as described. A deep pelmet box (front face plus returns at full depth) is made by adding a bottom strip and treating the construction as a three-sided timber box, which is considerably more rigid than a simple shelf-style board.

attrs.fix: Slacken the lower bracket fixing screws and pack behind the bracket with a thin timber shim until the board reads level. Re-tighten the screws.
attrs.cause: Bracket arms at different heights; fixing holes drilled at different vertical positions.

attrs.fix: Refit the board 25 to 50 mm higher. If the wall fixing will not allow this, change to a lower-profile track that sits closer to the wall.
attrs.cause: Board fixed too close to the top of the track; heading allowance insufficient.
attrs.symptom: Curtain heading catches on the underside of the pelmet board when drawn

attrs.fix: Add a third bracket at the midpoint. For boards longer than 1800 mm, three brackets are the minimum.
attrs.cause: Board too long for two brackets; 18 mm MDF spans too far without intermediate support.
attrs.symptom: Board sags in the centre over time

initialFindings.message: PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
